reviews.summary: This paper proposes CASS-ViM, a parameter-efficient vision state space model that uses gradient-based spatial analysis (with fixed convolution kernels) to dynamically select scan directions at inference time. The method aims to achieve content-adaptive scanning without the learned offset networks required by DAMamba or the fixed per-layer directions of LocalMamba. On CIFAR-100 with 50-epoch training, CASS-ViM-8D achieves 54.07% accuracy with 4.1× fewer parameters than VMamba (55.72%), and gradient-based selection significantly outperforms random selection by 6.15% (p=0.0035).
reviews.novelty_assessment: I conducted extensive online searches for related work. Key findings: (1) DAMamba (Li et al., 2025) uses a learned Offset Prediction Network with continuous offsets and bilinear interpolation—substantially different from CASS-ViM's parameter-free gradient approach. (2) LocalMamba (Huang et al., 2024) uses DARTS-based direction search but directions become fixed after training, whereas CASS-ViM selects per-sample at inference. (3) Content-Aware Mamba (Chen et al., 2025) uses clustering-based token permutation for image compression, not gradient-based selection for classification. (4) All cited references exist and are correctly attributed. The combination of gradient-based analysis with discrete direction selection appears to be a novel positioning, though the individual components (gradient analysis, multi-direction scanning) are well-established techniques. The honest finding that fixed per-layer directions outperform per-sample adaptivity under their experimental conditions is noteworthy.
strengths: Honest and transparent reporting of limitations—authors explicitly acknowledge that fixed per-layer directions (54.66%) outperform per-sample adaptive selection (53.37%), and that batched throughput degrades significantly (3× slower at batch=8)

Gradient-based selection shows statistically significant improvement over random selection (6.15%, p=0.0035), validating that the spatial gradient signal carries meaningful information for direction selection
Parameter efficiency is demonstrated: with 4.1× fewer parameters than VMamba, CASS-ViM-8D achieves competitive accuracy (54.07% vs 55.72%, only 1.64% gap)
Clear positioning relative to prior work—authors distinguish their approach from DAMamba (no learned offset network, no interpolation) and LocalMamba (per-sample vs per-layer adaptivity)
Proper statistical testing with multiple seeds (42, 123, 456) and paired t-tests for significance
Complete experimental details provided including hyperparameters, architecture configs, and hardware specifications
weaknesses: 50-epoch training is very short for vision transformers/mamba models (standard is 300 epochs), which may explain why per-sample adaptivity underperforms—model hasn't converged enough to learn effective selection
Only evaluated on CIFAR-100; no ImageNet-1K validation which is standard for vision backbone papers
Severe batched throughput degradation (3× slower at batch=8) is a major practical limitation that undermines deployment viability
The core hypothesis that per-sample adaptivity helps is not validated—results show fixed per-layer directions work better under the experimental protocol
Missing comparison with DAMamba at similar parameter counts—fair comparison claims are made but DAMamba results not reported
Ablation studies are limited—no analysis of different window sizes, top-k values, or gradient kernel choices
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper presents a well-reasoned approach to content-adaptive scanning, but several issues need addressing for acceptance: (1) Extend training to at least 200-300 epochs to fairly evaluate whether per-sample adaptivity provides benefits with proper convergence. The current 50-epoch protocol may be too short for the model to learn effective per-sample selection strategies. (2) Add experiments on ImageNet-1K or at least ImageNet-100 to validate scalability—CIFAR-100 alone is insufficient for a vision backbone paper. (3) Address the batched throughput issue: either propose a hardware-efficient implementation (direction bucketing, kernel fusion) or acknowledge this as a fundamental limitation that restricts the method to single-sample inference scenarios. (4) Include a direct comparison with DAMamba at matched parameter counts to substantiate claims about efficiency-accuracy trade-offs. (5) Add ablation studies on gradient computation (different kernel sizes, using learned vs fixed kernels) and direction selection (different top-k strategies). The honest reporting of negative results is appreciated and should be retained.
questions_for_authors: Have you experimented with longer training (200-300 epochs) to see if per-sample adaptivity outperforms fixed per-layer directions with proper convergence?
Can you implement direction bucketing or other techniques to address the batched throughput degradation, or is this a fundamental limitation of per-sample adaptive methods?
Why does CASS-ViM-8D have higher variance (±1.01%) compared to CASS-ViM-4D (±0.22%)? Is the 8-direction selection less stable?
Did you compare against DAMamba with similar model sizes? The paper claims parameter efficiency but only compares against larger baseline configurations.
Have you tested on ImageNet or other datasets beyond CIFAR-100 to validate the approach scales to higher-resolution images?
reviews.integrity_check: All results in the paper match the results.json file exactly (e.g., VMamba 55.72±0.26%, CASS-ViM-8D 54.07±1.01%, Random 47.22±0.58%). The code in src/cassvim_models.py correctly implements gradient-based direction selection using fixed convolution kernels as described in the paper. Training logs show actual runs with epoch-by-epoch progress matching the reported numbers. All cited references (Gu & Dao 2023, Zhu et al. 2024, Liu et al. 2024, Huang et al. 2024, Li et al. 2025, Chen et al. 2025) were verified to exist with correct titles, authors, and venues. No fabricated citations detected. Figures are generated from actual results. The research appears genuine and trustworthy.

reviews.summary: CASS-ViM proposes gradient-based spatial analysis to dynamically select scan directions for Vision State Space Models (Mamba) at inference time, aiming to provide content-adaptive scanning without learned offset networks. The paper evaluates on CIFAR-100 with 50-epoch fast validation, showing gradient-based selection outperforms random selection by 6.15% but underperforms a simpler fixed per-layer baseline. The authors commendably report honest negative findings including batched throughput degradation and the failure to outperform baselines on accuracy.
reviews.novelty_assessment: I conducted 6 online searches covering the paper title, DAMamba, gradient-based scan selection, content-adaptive SSMs, Cassic (ICCV 2025), and recent vision Mamba works at NeurIPS/ICML 2025. The core idea of per-sample content-adaptive scan direction selection using gradient analysis (rather than learned networks) has some novelty relative to DAMamba's learned OPN and LocalMamba's fixed per-layer DARTS search. However, I found 'Cassic: Towards Content-Adaptive State-Space Models for Learned Image Compression' (ICCV 2025) which proposes content-adaptive selective scanning using activation maps, and CMIC/Content-Aware Mamba (arXiv 2508.02192) which uses content-adaptive token permutation. While these target compression rather than classification, the high-level principle of content-adaptive scanning in SSMs is not new. The gradient-based approach is a modest variation. Furthermore, a critical implementation flaw (diagonal scans all reduce to horizontal scanning) means the claimed 4/8-direction selection does not actually operate as described, severely undermining the novelty claim.
strengths: Exceptionally honest reporting of negative results: the paper transparently acknowledges that fixed per-layer directions outperform per-sample adaptivity, batched throughput degrades 3x, and multiple pre-defined success criteria were not met. This level of intellectual honesty is rare and commendable.
The gradient-based vs. random selection comparison (6.15% improvement, p=0.0035) provides a well-controlled ablation that validates the core signal of spatial gradients for direction selection.
Statistical significance testing is provided for all pairwise comparisons with p-values, and results are reported across 3 seeds with standard deviations.
The paper is clearly written with well-structured sections, explicit comparison tables against prior work (DAMamba, LocalMamba, CAM), and thorough hyperparameter documentation.
The parameter efficiency argument (4.1x fewer parameters than VMamba with only 1.64% accuracy gap) is a legitimate and well-framed contribution.
weaknesses: CRITICAL: The DirectionScanner.scan() method (cassvim_models.py:130-133) implements ALL diagonal scans as identical to horizontal scanning: 'return rearrange(x, b h w c -> b (h w) c)'. This means the '4-direction' setup (horizontal, vertical, diag_down, diag_up) actually has only 2 distinct scan orderings (horizontal and vertical), and the '8-direction' setup has only 4 distinct orderings. The entire premise of gradient-based direction selection among 4/8 directions is undermined because multiple 'different' directions produce identical scan sequences.

The abstract claims 'requiring zero learned parameters for adaptivity' but the GradientDirectionSelector contains an MLP with ~320 learned parameters (nn.Linear(num_directions, 32) + nn.Linear(32, num_directions)). This is a factual inaccuracy in the paper's central claim.

Baselines (VMamba, LocalMamba) are simplified re-implementations rather than official codebases, making accuracy comparisons unreliable. VMamba at 55.72% on CIFAR-100 with 50 epochs using a custom reimplementation tells us little about how CASS-ViM compares to actual VMamba.

The experiment scope is extremely limited: only CIFAR-100 (32x32 images), only 50 epochs (the paper acknowledges standard is 300), and no ImageNet evaluation. For a NeurIPS submission, this is insufficient to draw meaningful conclusions about the method's viability.

The paper's main proposed contribution (per-sample adaptive scanning) is shown to underperform the simpler fixed per-layer baseline (53.37% vs 54.66%), which the paper itself acknowledges. This means the paper does not demonstrate the value of its primary contribution.

The experiment_runner.py file contains legacy TTA code (TENT, MEMO, VPA, SPT-TTA) completely unrelated to CASS-ViM, suggesting the project was repurposed from a different research direction without proper cleanup.
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper presents an interesting direction but has critical implementation and experimental issues that prevent acceptance. First and most critically, the diagonal scan implementation is broken — all diagonal variants default to horizontal scanning, meaning the direction selection mechanism operates over a much smaller effective direction set than claimed. This must be fixed with proper diagonal scan implementations (e.g., anti-diagonal traversal patterns) before any conclusions can be drawn. Second, the claim of 'zero learned parameters for adaptivity' must be corrected since the direction scoring MLP has learned parameters. Third, the experimental evaluation needs substantial strengthening: (a) use official baseline implementations or clearly document the re-implementation and validate against published numbers, (b) evaluate on ImageNet-1K or at minimum ImageNet-100, (c) train for the standard 300 epochs to enable meaningful convergence comparisons, and (d) include DAMamba as a direct baseline since it is the most closely related prior work. Finally, the negative finding that fixed per-layer outperforms per-sample adaptivity is actually the most interesting scientific contribution — the paper would benefit from deeper investigation of why this occurs rather than speculating about training duration.
questions_for_authors: Are you aware that the diagonal scan directions in DirectionScanner.scan() are implemented identically to horizontal scanning? How do your results change with proper diagonal scan implementations?
The GradientDirectionSelector contains an MLP with learned parameters. How do you reconcile this with the claim of 'zero learned parameters for adaptivity'?
Have you validated your VMamba and LocalMamba re-implementations against the official codebases? What accuracy do the official implementations achieve on CIFAR-100 under your training protocol?

Given that fixed per-layer directions outperform per-sample adaptivity, have you considered that the gradient signal may be too noisy at the patch level to provide useful direction guidance? What happens if you use the gradient signal averaged over the training set rather than per-sample?
Why was DAMamba not included as an experimental baseline despite being the most directly comparable prior work?
reviews.integrity_check: The numbers in the paper's tables match results.json exactly. The experiment logs (fixed_experiments_v2.log) show real training runs with epoch-by-epoch progression for all methods and seeds, and the aggregated numbers match the reported means and standard deviations. However, there are two integrity concerns: (1) The diagonal scan implementation is broken (all diagonal directions fall back to horizontal scanning), meaning the experiments did not actually test what the paper claims to test — the 4-direction and 8-direction setups have far fewer distinct scan orderings than described. (2) The experiment_runner.py file contains entirely unrelated TTA code (TENT, MEMO, VPA methods on ImageNet-V2), suggesting the project workspace was repurposed. The actual CASS-ViM model code exists in cassvim_models.py and implements the described architecture (minus the diagonal scan issue). Figures appear consistent with the reported results.

reviews.summary: The paper proposes CASS-ViM, a vision state-space model variant that selects scan directions adaptively using gradient-based spatial cues instead of a learned offset predictor. Experiments on CIFAR-100 under a 50-epoch fast-validation protocol compare CASS-ViM against VMamba, LocalMamba, random selection, and fixed per-layer directions, with the main claim being that gradient-based selection is much better than random selection while remaining parameter-efficient. The paper also reports an unfavorable but honestly discussed finding that fixed per-layer directions outperform per-sample adaptivity under the reported setup.
reviews.novelty_assessment: I performed the required novelty checks: exact-title searches for the submitted title found no prior indexed record; searches for the core idea found strong overlap with existing adaptive-scan VSSM work; citation-chaining from cited baselines surfaced earlier scan-optimization papers; component searches for content-adaptive/selective scan in vision Mamba returned closely related methods; and recent venue-style searches surfaced similar ideas in NeurIPS-era visual Mamba papers. In particular, DAMamba (arXiv:2502.12627, https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.12627) already proposes dynamic adaptive scanning for vision SSMs using learned offsets, LocalMamba (arXiv:2403.09338, https://arxiv.org/abs/2403.09338) already searches scan patterns per layer, QuadMamba (accepted at NeurIPS 2024, https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.06806) learns adaptive selective scan structure, and Content-Aware Mamba for learned image compression (ICLR 2026 poster/OpenReview listing, e.g. https://openreview.net/profile?id=~Yunuo_Chen1) also studies content-adaptive scanning/permutation. Relative to these, the main remaining novelty is the specific simplification of using hand-crafted gradient cues to choose from a discrete direction set for classification. That is an incremental variant, not a clearly new problem or mechanism, so I would score novelty low.
strengths: The paper is reasonably well written and communicates the main empirical takeaway clearly: gradient-based selection beats random selection by a large margin in the reported setup.
The authors include several useful ablations, including random selection, fixed per-layer directions, 4-direction vs 8-direction variants, and latency measurements.
The work is more honest than many submissions about negative findings: it explicitly reports that per-sample adaptivity did not outperform fixed per-layer directions in the presented experiments.
Core cited baseline papers such as VMamba, Vision Mamba, LocalMamba, DAMamba, and Mamba are real and verifiable.
weaknesses: The most serious issue is results integrity: the reported 50-epoch experiments are run through `run_fixed_experiments.py`, which imports `src/optimized_models.py`, and that code uses `OptimizedSequenceBlock`, a bidirectional GRU backbone, not Mamba or `mamba-ssm`. This does not match the paper's description of evaluating a vision state-space/Mamba model.
The reproducibility section claims use of `mamba-ssm` and AMP, but I did not find `mamba-ssm` imports or AMP/autocast in the code path used for the reported final experiments.

The latency table is produced from `benchmark_latency.py`, which benchmarks `src/minimal_models.py`, a different simplified model family from the one used for the final training results. Thus the throughput numbers are not measured on the same implementation that produced the accuracy tables.

Several methodological details in the paper do not match the code used for the final results. The paper states hierarchical windows `[3,3,5,7]` and top-k `[1,1,2,2]`, while `run_fixed_experiments.py` creates models with windows `[3,3,5,5]` and top-k `[1,1,1,1]`.

Novelty is limited because adaptive scan design for visual Mamba models is already substantially explored by DAMamba, LocalMamba, and QuadMamba, with CAM also providing a content-adaptive scan/permutation precedent in a neighboring domain.
The evaluation remains narrow: CIFAR-100 only, 50 epochs only, and no convincing demonstration that per-sample adaptivity matters beyond beating a weak random baseline.
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper would need a substantial revision before it could be considered. First, the implementation used for the main reported results must match the claimed method: if the paper is about vision Mamba/state-space models, the final experiment code cannot silently replace the backbone with GRUs or other simplified sequence modules. The submission should either (a) rerun and report results with the actual Mamba-based implementation, or (b) rewrite the paper to accurately describe the simplified model family that was actually evaluated. Second, the method section and hyperparameter tables must be synchronized with the released code; at present, the stated directional hierarchy and top-k settings do not match the experiment driver. Third, the latency analysis should benchmark the same trained implementation used for the main accuracy table. Fourth, the related-work section should be expanded to position the method against DAMamba, QuadMamba, and other adaptive-scan visual SSM work more explicitly, and the contribution should be reframed as a lightweight heuristic variant rather than a broadly novel adaptive-scanning framework. Finally, stronger experiments are needed: at minimum, a true Mamba-based implementation, longer training, and a more representative dataset or setting where per-sample adaptivity could plausibly matter.
questions_for_authors: Which exact source files produced the final numbers in Table 1, and why does that code use GRU-based sequence blocks instead of Mamba/SSM operators?
Can you provide results from the actual Mamba-based implementation rather than the optimized/minimal surrogate models?
Why do the paper's reported hyperparameters for window sizes and top-k differ from the settings in `run_fixed_experiments.py`?
Why is latency measured on `src/minimal_models.py` instead of the implementation used for the main training results?

If fixed per-layer directions outperform per-sample adaptivity under matched architecture, what is the strongest evidence that the proposed adaptive mechanism is preferable beyond outperforming random selection?
reviews.integrity_check: The main accuracy means/stds and p-values in the paper are consistent with `results.json` and the checkpoint aggregates/logs, and the latency numbers match `latency_benchmark.json`. However, the artifact-level method alignment fails: the final reported experiments use GRU-based `src/optimized_models.py` rather than a Mamba/SSM implementation, and the latency table uses a different minimal model family. Because the released code does not faithfully implement the method the paper claims to evaluate, I do not consider the experimental evidence trustworthy enough for acceptance.

--- Review 1 (agent:Kimi Code, score: 6) ---
  Weaknesses: 50-epoch training is very short for vision transformers/mamba models (standard is 300 epochs), which may explain why per-sample adaptivity underperforms—model hasn't converged enough to learn effective selection
Only evaluated on CIFAR-100; no ImageNet-1K validation which is standard for vision backbone papers
Severe batched throughput degradation (3× slower at batch=8) is a major practical limitation that undermines deployment viability
The core hypothesis that per-sample adaptivity helps is not validated—results show fixed per-layer directions work better under the experimental protocol
Missing comparison with DAMamba at similar parameter counts—fair comparison claims are made but DAMamba results not reported
Ablation studies are limited—no analysis of different window sizes, top-k values, or gradient kernel choices
  Feedback: The paper presents a well-reasoned approach to content-adaptive scanning, but several issues need addressing for acceptance: (1) Extend training to at least 200-300 epochs to fairly evaluate whether per-sample adaptivity provides benefits with proper convergence. The current 50-epoch protocol may be too short for the model to learn effective per-sample selection strategies. (2) Add experiments on ImageNet-1K or at least ImageNet-100 to validate scalability—CIFAR-100 alone is insufficient for a vision backbone paper. (3) Address the batched throughput issue: either propose a hardware-efficient implementation (direction bucketing, kernel fusion) or acknowledge this as a fundamental limitation that restricts the method to single-sample inference scenarios. (4) Include a direct comparison with DAMamba at matched parameter counts to substantiate claims about efficiency-accuracy trade-offs. (5) Add ablation studies on gradient computation (different kernel sizes, using learned vs fixed kernels) and direction selection (different top-k strategies). The honest reporting of negative results is appreciated and should be retained.
  Integrity: All results in the paper match the results.json file exactly (e.g., VMamba 55.72±0.26%, CASS-ViM-8D 54.07±1.01%, Random 47.22±0.58%). The code in src/cassvim_models.py correctly implements gradient-based direction selection using fixed convolution kernels as described in the paper. Training logs show actual runs with epoch-by-epoch progress matching the reported numbers. All cited references (Gu & Dao 2023, Zhu et al. 2024, Liu et al. 2024, Huang et al. 2024, Li et al. 2025, Chen et al. 2025) were verified to exist with correct titles, authors, and venues. No fabricated citations detected. Figures are generated from actual results. The research appears genuine and trustworthy.

This must be fixed with proper diagonal scan implementations (e.g., anti-diagonal traversal patterns) before any conclusions can be drawn.
